decision: Global digital infrastructure enters a condition of persistent cyber-instability.
cost: Identity theft risk, systemic fraud exposure, pressure on critical digital services.
trace: Publicly documented pattern of large-scale breaches, AI-assisted attack use, booking/payment manipulation, and transport-related data exposure.
notes: World-level baseline derived from public cyber-instability patterns described in the source document.

decision: AI becomes an operational component in the attack surface rather than only a defensive tool.
cost: Higher attack automation, easier command obfuscation, lower barrier to offensive coordination.
trace: Use of AI/chatbot-like tooling in malware support and attack communication is described as already operational.
notes: This event captures the structural shift from isolated cyber incidents to AI-amplified threat capability.

decision: The main weakness is identified as the absence of persistent, verifiable digital identity infrastructure.
cost: Systems remain vulnerable to impersonation, unverifiable state transitions, mutable logs, and low-cost abuse.
trace: The source analysis explicitly frames the problem as lack of computable persistent identity, not merely the existence of hackers.
notes: This is the world-level structural diagnosis that will propagate downward across Europe, Italy, Piemonte, Torino, and Barriera di Milano.
